primary_diagnosis.name: Advanced gastric adenocarcinoma with acute upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage and hypovolaemia (metastatic — Virchow's node and Sister Mary Joseph nodule)

comorbidities.name: Acute upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage (bleeding ulcerated gastric tumour)
Hypovolaemia / early hypovolaemic shock from blood loss
Cancer cachexia with severe unintentional weight loss

pathophysiology_summary: An ulcerating gastric adenocarcinoma has eroded into the gastric mucosal vasculature, producing an acute upper-gastrointestinal bleed — manifest as coffee-ground haematemesis (partially digested blood) and melaena (digested blood in stool). Ongoing blood loss is causing hypovolaemia with a compensatory tachycardia (HR 112) and a falling blood pressure (95/60), the early stage of haemorrhagic shock; the conjunctival pallor reflects chronic anaemia from indolent blood loss and marrow suppression. The underlying malignancy explains the alarm constellation the note describes as classically defined by persistent PPI-refractory dyspepsia, early satiety with unintentional weight loss, and a Virchow's (left supraclavicular) node — with a palpable epigastric mass and a periumbilical Sister Mary Joseph nodule signalling transcoelomic/lymphatic metastatic spread. The five-month PPI-refractory course is the key alarm feature: dyspepsia that does not respond to adequate acid suppression, together with weight loss and early satiety in an older adult, mandates urgent endoscopy to exclude gastric cancer. Definitive diagnosis requires endoscopy with biopsy and staging imaging — none of which is available via telehealth; the telehealth task is to recognise the active bleed and the alarm pattern and escalate, not to confirm the cancer.

differential.evidence_basis: Older adult with months of PPI-refractory epigastric pain, marked unintentional weight loss and early satiety, now with coffee-ground haematemesis — a classic upper-GI cancer alarm constellation complicated by an acute bleed.
The most common cause of coffee-ground haematemesis and melaena; must be actively considered, but does not by itself explain the profound weight loss and metastatic signs.
Another cause of upper-GI bleeding to keep in mind, though no chronic liver disease / portal hypertension features are reported.

The anchoring trap: five months of PPI-refractory pain with weight loss, early satiety and now haematemesis is NOT benign reflux or functional dyspepsia — these alarm features exclude a benign functional label.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the two non-negotiables are (a) treating the coffee-ground vomit as an active upper-GI bleed and (b) NOT closing on benign chronic dyspepsia despite the long history.

differential.diagnosis: Bleeding gastric malignancy with metastatic disease
differential.evidence_basis: Weight loss + early satiety + PPI-refractory pain + melaena + coffee-ground haematemesis together point strongly to an ulcerated gastric malignancy; palpable Virchow's node and Sister Mary Joseph nodule (clinician exam) confirm metastatic spread.
Remains the leading benign cause of the bleed and cannot be excluded without endoscopy; management of the acute bleed is the same regardless — resuscitate and scope.
Haematemesis and melaena point to a mucosal gastric source, not isolated pancreatic exocrine failure.
Constant deep pain, cachexia and active bleeding are not features of isolated delayed gastric emptying without structural disease.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the correct posture is dual: manage the acute haemorrhage as an emergency AND recognise a probable upper-GI malignancy requiring urgent endoscopy/specialist referral.

differential.diagnosis: Advanced gastric adenocarcinoma with acute upper-GI haemorrhage (metastatic)
differential.evidence_basis: Full constellation with haemodynamic instability; definitive confirmation (endoscopy + biopsy + staging) is a hospital task, but the emergency bleed and the malignancy alarm pattern are both recognisable now and both mandate escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm the cancer; it can and must recognise the active bleed with shock signs and escalate to 000, while flagging the need for urgent endoscopy and specialist upper-GI/oncology assessment.

red_flags.description: Coffee-ground haematemesis — active upper gastrointestinal bleeding
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals ongoing upper-GI blood loss; with haemodynamic compromise this is a time-critical emergency that can progress to exsanguinating haemorrhage.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undetected ongoing bleeding leads to hypovolaemic shock and death.

red_flags.description: Haemodynamic instability — hypotension (BP 95/60) and tachycardia (HR 112) with profound weakness and lightheadedness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Early haemorrhagic (hypovolaemic) shock; the measured BP/HR are clinician-obtained but the patient-reportable weakness and lightheadedness are the telehealth proxy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to decompensated shock and cardiac arrest.

red_flags.description: Melaena — black tarry stools indicating a digested upper-GI blood source
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms upper-GI haemorrhage; found on DRE by the clinician but also patient-observable on direct questioning about stool colour.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to recognise the bleed's severity and delayed resuscitation.

red_flags.description: Malignancy alarm cluster — PPI-refractory epigastric pain, marked unintentional weight loss (~12 kg) and early satiety in an older adult
red_flags.clinical_significance: Upper-GI cancer alarm features mandating urgent endoscopy; failing to recognise them is the classic premature-closure error for chronic dyspepsia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed or delayed cancer diagnosis; the patient reassured as benign reflux.

red_flags.description: Palpable metastatic disease — firm irregular epigastric mass, fixed left supraclavicular (Virchow's) node, periumbilical (Sister Mary Joseph) nodule
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signs of advanced/metastatic malignancy; require clinician palpation and cannot be elicited via telehealth, but confirm the seriousness once assessed in person.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciation of disease stage; but does not change the immediate T5 escalation for the bleed.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on chronic dyspepsia / benign GORD
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being reassured by the long five-month history and a prior 'stomach tablet' prescription, and closing on benign reflux or functional dyspepsia — ignoring that the pain is PPI-refractory with weight loss and early satiety, which are alarm features.
Attributing the weight loss to age or poor appetite and deferring to a routine GP review, rather than recognising an upper-GI cancer alarm cluster requiring urgent endoscopy.
Under-weighting the coffee-ground vomit and melaena and failing to treat this as an active upper-GI haemorrhage with early shock — the immediate life threat.
Advising the patient to take an antacid, eat/drink, or drive themselves to hospital, instead of nil-by-mouth plus an emergency ambulance for a haemodynamically unstable bleed.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Actual blood pressure and heart rate (clinician-measured; 95/60 and 112 in the source)
Abdominal palpation — the firm irregular epigastric mass and tenderness
Left supraclavicular (Virchow's) node and periumbilical (Sister Mary Joseph) nodule
Haemoglobin, full blood count, urea, coagulation and group-and-hold bloods
Upper GI endoscopy (the ulcerated gastric mass and its bleeding point) and biopsy
CT staging / cross-sectional imaging and tumour markers
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise an active upper-GI bleed with signs of early shock and escalate immediately: call 000 for an emergency ambulance, keep the patient nil-by-mouth, lay them flat, and stay with them — do not defer to a routine 2-week-wait pathway. Separately flag that the alarm features mandate urgent inpatient endoscopy and specialist upper-GI/oncology assessment.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Cancer cannot be confirmed remotely and must not be asserted as fact to the patient; the real risk is UNDER-action — reassuring the chronic-dyspepsia narrative, missing the acute bleed, or downgrading to a routine referral instead of a T5 emergency.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE NG12 — Suspected cancer: recognition and referral
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Refer urgently (2-week-wait / direct-access endoscopy) adults with dyspepsia plus weight loss, and any upper-abdominal mass consistent with stomach cancer; treatment-refractory dyspepsia with alarm features warrants urgent endoscopy.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE NG141 / BSG — Acute upper gastrointestinal bleeding
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Patients with acute upper-GI bleeding and haemodynamic instability need immediate resuscitation and urgent (within 24 h) endoscopy; risk-stratify but do not delay resuscitation and transfer.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Cancer Council Australia — Optimal Care Pathway, oesophago-gastric cancer
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Alarm features (unintentional weight loss, dysphagia, GI bleeding, epigastric mass, persistent vomiting) require prompt endoscopic assessment and specialist multidisciplinary referral.
